md: A **reflection** flips a shape across a line — the **line of reflection** (or **mirror line**). Every point of the pre-image has an image directly opposite it on the other side of the mirror, at the same perpendicular distance.
Fold the paper along the mirror line and the pre-image lands exactly on its image.

The red dashed line is the mirror. Switch the axis in the dropdown and watch the image jump to its new position. Notice the arrow flips direction — that's the **orientation reversal** that distinguishes reflections from translations and rotations.

blocks.title: The four basic reflection rules
blocks.body:
Memorize these — they cover most problems:

**Over the y-axis:** $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$
**Over the x-axis:** $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$
**Over the line $y = x$:** $(x, y) \to (y, x)$
**Over the line $y = -x$:** $(x, y) \to (-y, -x)$

md: **Over the y-axis.** Every point $(x, y)$ has its mirror image at the same height $y$, with the x-coordinate flipped in sign. So $(x, y) \to (-x, y)$.
**Over the x-axis.** Same idea: keep $x$, flip $y$. So $(x, y) \to (x, -y)$.
**Over $y = x$.** This line treats $x$ and $y$ symmetrically. Swapping them puts you on the mirror image. So $(x, y) \to (y, x)$.
**Preserved:** distances, angles, side lengths, area, shape.

**Flipped:** orientation. A clockwise polygon becomes counterclockwise (and vice versa). This is why reflections are sometimes called **opposite isometries** — they're rigid motions, but they reverse handedness.
Try this in the widget: pick a non-symmetric shape (like the arrow, default). Reflect it. The arrow points the other way.

blocks.title: Double reflection = translation or rotation
blocks.body:
Reflect twice over the same line: you get the identity (back to where you started).

Reflect over two **parallel** lines: the result is a **translation** by twice the distance between the lines.

Reflect over two **intersecting** lines: the result is a **rotation** about their intersection point by twice the angle between them.

These are deep facts — every rigid motion in the plane is at most three reflections.

md: Sanity check: midpoint of $AA'$ should lie on the mirror $y = x$. Midpoint $= ((3 + -2)/2,\; (-2 + 3)/2) = (0.5, 0.5)$. Yes — on $y = x$. ✓
Also, the segment $AA'$ should be perpendicular to $y = x$ (slope = 1), so $AA'$ should have slope $-1$. Slope of $AA' = (3 - (-2))/((-2) - 3) = 5/(-5) = -1$. ✓
